coredata.dc:title: Design and green synthesis of carbon Dots/Gold nanoparticles Composites and their applications for neurotransmitters sensing based on emission Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: El-Said, Waleed A.
Akhdhar, Abdullah
Al-Bogami, Abdullah S.
Saleh, Tamer S.
coredata.dc:description: Changes in the neurotransmitters are indications for several diseases. Several sensors were reported for monitoring dopamine (DA), but the simple and accurate DA detection in biological samples still faces many challenges. The research proposal aims to develop an optical sensor for detecting neurotransmitters based on luminescence emission spectra in different biological samples. Carbon dots (CDs) were fabricated based on a green synthesis route. Then the prepared CDs were decorated with varying concentrations of gold nanoparticles (Au NPs). The synthesis process was optimized, and the obtained CDs/Au NPs nanocomposites were applied as neurotransmitters’ optical nanosensors. The optical nanosensor approach provides easy and sensitive multiplex analysis. A wide range of neurotransmitters was monitored. The developed sensor’s sensitivity, selectivity, and reproducibility were investigated. Au NPs act as CDs’ stabilizers, enhancing the emission effect, and scaffolds for binding DA with CDs’ surface. DA moieties bind to CDs through the interaction between the DA-NH2 groups and Au NPS. Due to electron transfer, the bonding of DA molecules leads to fluorescence quenching of AuNPs/CDs. The Au-CDs-based DA fluorescence showed high sensitivity with adetection limit, and limit quantification of 2.04 nM and 6.18 nM, respectively. Furthermore, the selectivity of the sensor was investigated in the presence of glucose, uric acid (UA), and ascorbic acid (AA), which showed no interference effect at 10 times higher concentrations. Moreover, the proposed sensor has been successfully utilized for DA detection in human serum samples with a high recovery efficiency between 98.83 % and 103.5 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Green synthesis
Neurotransmitters
Dopamine
Fluorescence quenching
Au NPs/CDs

DA sensing and real sample application
Characterization of the Au NPs-CDs nanocomposites
Optical properties of CDs and Au-CDs
Selectivity and reproducibility of Au NPs-CDs toward detection of DA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of CDs, (b) TEM image of CDs, and (c) SEM image of Au NPs-CDs.
(a) UV–vis spectrum of CDs, (b) UV–vis spectrum of different Au1-4@CDs, and (c) photograph of the different Au1-4-CDs and the CDs solution.
Emission spectra of (a) CDs, and (b) different Au1-4@CDs.
(a) emission spectra of CDs upon interaction with different concentrations of DA, and (b) relationship between the dopamine concentrations and the (F0/F) −1.

(a) emission spectra of DA after interacting with different Au1-4@CDs exited with aleaser of 240 nm, (b) emission spectra of Au NPs-CDs upon interaction with different concentrations of DA, and (c) the relationship between the dopamine concentrations and the (F0/F) −1.
(a) Monitoring of DA by using Au NPs-CDs in the presence of AA, UA, and glucose, and (b) emission spectra of DA after interacting with three batches of Au NPs-CDs.
Recovery efficiency of different concentrations of DA spiked in human serum solution.
Schematic design of DA sensing based on the green synthesized Au NPs-CDs.
Comparison of different absorption and emission-based DA sensors.
Recoveries percentage of DA spiked in human serum solution.

head.ce:title: Design and green synthesis of carbon Dots/Gold nanoparticles Composites and their applications for neurotransmitters sensing based on emission Spectroscopy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Jeddah, P.O. Box 80327, Jeddah 21589, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Jeddah, P.O. Box 80327, Jeddah 21589, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Changes in the neurotransmitters are indications for several diseases. Several sensors were reported for monitoring dopamine (DA), but the simple and accurate DA detection in biological samples still faces many challenges. The research proposal aims to develop an optical sensor for detecting neurotransmitters based on luminescence emission spectra in different biological samples. Carbon dots (CDs) were fabricated based on a green synthesis route. Then the prepared CDs were decorated with varying concentrations of gold nanoparticles (Au NPs). The synthesis process was optimized, and the obtained CDs/Au NPs nanocomposites were applied as neurotransmitters’ optical nanosensors. The optical nanosensor approach provides easy and sensitive multiplex analysis. A wide range of neurotransmitters was monitored. The developed sensor’s sensitivity, selectivity, and reproducibility were investigated. Au NPs act as CDs’ stabilizers, enhancing the emission effect, and scaffolds for binding DA with CDs’ surface. DA moieties bind to CDs through the interaction between the DA-NH2 groups and Au NPS. Due to electron transfer, the bonding of DA molecules leads to fluorescence quenching of AuNPs/CDs. The Au-CDs-based DA fluorescence showed high sensitivity with adetection limit, and limit quantification of 2.04 nM and 6.18 nM, respectively. Furthermore, the selectivity of the sensor was investigated in the presence of glucose, uric acid (UA), and ascorbic acid (AA), which showed no interference effect at 10 times higher concentrations. Moreover, the proposed sensor has been successfully utilized for DA detection in human serum samples with a high recovery efficiency between 98.83 % and 103.5 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: all data were shared with the manuscript

ce:section.ce:para: Neurotransmitters are a group of chemicals, that act as messengersbetween the neurons, and stimulate the responses from muscle cells and glandular [1–6]. Dopamine (DA) is among the most important neurotransmitter families. DA displays several important functions in most of the human body systems including the brain, the neural system cardiovascular, besides the hormonal systems and behavioral responses [1–6]. The reported DA level in the human body ranges from 0.5 nM to 100 nM based on the type of fluids [7]. The variation of the neurotransmitters' level is used as an indicator for several diseases e.g. cancer, and neurological disorders including Alzheimer’s Parkinson’s, Schizophrenia, epilepsy, euphoria, and memory loss [8–10]. Thus, developing a sensitive, selective, simple, and accurate method for DA-level monitoring is extremely needed for mental disease diagnosis and treatment. Various techniques were reported for monitoring the change of the neurotransmitters' levels in either in-vivo or in-vitro systems [11–13]. Electrochemistry [14–16] enzymatic methods [17] spectrophotometry [18–20], and chromatography-based techniques [21,22] were reported for DA detection. However, the majority of these techniques need expensive equipment, time-consuming, complicated, and laborious [23]. Therefore, several studies are conducted to obtain accurate, and cost-effective methods [24–27]. The electrochemical and optical are the widest applied techniques for neurotransmitter detection due to their unique features [28–31].

Electrochemical techniques possess many advantages including high speed of response, specificity, the use of relatively inexpensive equipment, and high sensitivity for neurotransmitter detection [32–35]. Althoughthe electrochemical DA sensing methods have advantages, they suffer from theinterfacial effects of uric acid and ascorbic acid, which have almost the same DA oxidation potential [36]. Colorimetric sensors were used for DA monitoring based on color changes [36–42]. Several chemicals, carbon dots, Ag NPs, or DNA aptamers modified Au NPs were used for DA sensing. However, there is a need for developing more sensitive and accurate methods for DA monitoring.

Among the optical techniques, the fluorescence technique has high sensitivity besides its ease of use. The optical fluorescence materials possessing appropriate functionalities have consistently demonstrated their potential for qualitative and quantitative analysis of the species [4,36]. The development of nanomaterials could enhance the efficiency of DA detection [43–46].

The worldwide imperative for a more environmentally friendly and enduring economy has impacted several sectors, such as nanotechnology and materials. The field of green synthesis of nanomaterials has recently gained attention as a promising area of research aimed at developing dependable, sustainable, and ecologically neutral materials and nanomaterials. By focusing on a more sustainable approach, nanotechnology can surpass the limitations of traditional methods, thus contributing to the development of a more sustainable and safer future. Upon their unexpected discovery in early 2004, carbon dots (CDs) have exhibited remarkable properties including environmental friendliness, ease of synthesis, nonblinking, high biocompatibility, electrochemiluminescence, durability, high photostability, exceptional multi-photon excitation (up or down-conversion) capacity, ease of functionalization, and solubility in water with a high carbon content (up to 99.9 %) [47–51]. CDs and other carbon-based luminous nanomaterials are increasingly used as effective substitutes for organic dyes and heavy metal-based semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) like CdSe and PbS. This is because of their wide range of applications in bioimaging, biosensing, photonics, optoelectronics, and other technologies [52]. In addition, passivation makes the nitrogen-doped carbon dots more photoluminescent [53].

The QDs-based sensors showed significant advantages compared to the traditional detection systems, especially regarding their sensitivity and selectivity. QDs were used for DA detecting based on quenching the QDs' fluorescence based on the non-radiative electron-hole recombination annihilation [52,53]. Thus, several fluorescence probes were designed for DA detection.

However, there are several challenges to using QDs as DA sensors, including the toxicity of the QDs. Furthermore, the QDs are not water soluble and need surface modification to make them water soluble. These challenges restricted the applications of biosensors, especially in in-vivo applications. Accordingly, converting the QDs into a hydrophilic material is essential for using them QDs as probes for biomolecule sensing. Therefore, several approaches were used to make the QDs water-soluble. Phase transfer fabrication route was reported to obtain high-quality water-soluble QDs [54]. Furthermore, modification of the oil-soluble QDs with thiol ligands, nucleotides, or nucleosides was also reported to convert them into water-soluble QDs [55–57]. However, this modification could affect their fluorescence efficiency [58]. Therefore, there is a need to develop biocompatible materials with high-emission properties in aqueous mediums.

Carbon dots (CDs) with high emissions intensity in the aqueous medium are a good option. However, the chemical synthesis of CDs is normally performed in organic solvents, which could affect their biocompatibility. In our previous work, CDs/ZnO nanosensors were fabricated and used as sensors for hydrogen peroxide released from cancer cells [59]. This work aims to develop an optical diagnostic platform for neurotransmitter diagnosis using CDs/Au composite for detecting low concentrations of neurotransmitters in different matrixes. The principle of DA sensing using Au NPs-CDs is exemplified in Scheme 1 . Here, CDs were synthesized based on a green route in an aqueous medium. Furthermore, these CDs were modified with Au NPs to enhance their emission properties and act as a surface for binding the DA molecules with the CDs. Au NPs were used for coating the CDs to serve as binding sites to attach DA with CDs via the attraction between the amino groups of DA moieties and Au NPs. The attachment of DA to the CDs' surface led to the quenching of their luminescence through electron transfer. The luminescent intensity is quantitatively dependent on DA amount; thus, Au NPs/CDs could be used for DA detection. The obtained sensor was used for detecting a wide concentration range of DA neurotransmitters in different samples.
Dopamine hydrochloride, AA, glucose, uric acid, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7), human serum, sodium borohydride, gold chloride, and gold chloride were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA).

Fresh pomegranate juice was prepared without water and the seeds were filtered off. The pure juice was transferred to a Teflon-coated autoclave for hydrothermal treatment. The autoclave was kept for 6 h at 180 °C and then left for naturally cooling to normal room temperature. The formed CDs were obtained by centrifuge at 6000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was diluted with DIW and stored in the dark at 4 °C until further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the optical DA sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Four Au1-4/CDs were synthesized based on the gold salt reduction in the presence of CDs using NaBH4 as a reducing agent. Typically, different volumes (0.25, 0.5, 0,75, and 1 mL) of HAuCl4 (10 mM) were mixed with 10 mL CDs solution and stirred at 1000 rpm for 10 min. Then, 1 mL of chilled NaBH4 (1 mM) solution was added dropwise to the previous solution. A turnoff color from brown to reddish brown was observed. The fabricated Au1-4@CDs solutions were kept in the dark at 4 °C until further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DA sensing and real sample application
ce:section.ce:para: The human serum (HS) solution was prepared by dissolving the equivalent amount of HS sample in PBS (pH 7). Serial concentrations of DA were spiked in the HS solution. The DA content was directly measured using fluorescent analysis without any pretreatments.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a quartz cuvette with a path length of 1 cm at the Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). The analysis was recorded with λex at 245 nm and λem between 250 and 700 nm (5 nm bandwidth).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the Au NPs-CDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the CDs and CDs/Au nanocomposite were studied by using the SEM. To record the SEM images a few drops of CDs or CDs/Au nanocomposite solutions were added to the holder's surface and allowed to evaporate. Fig. 1 a shows the SEM image of CDs, which showed the formation of CDs with irregular morphology. The accumulation of the CDs during the SEM analysis made it difficult to measure their particle sizes. Therefore, TEM was used to investigate the morphology of the CDs. Fig. 1b shows the TEM image of the CDs, which illustrated the formation of irregular structures with a diameter of 5 nm. Furthermore, the morphology of the Au NPs-CDs nanocomposite was represented in Fig. 1C, which showed the deposition of Au NPs over the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDs and Au-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption of the CDs before and after being modified with Au NPs was studied before conducting the fluorescence measurement. Fig. 2 a represents the UV–vis spectra of pure CDs and Au-CDs within the wavelength range from 200 nm to 700 nm. The UV–vis spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2a) showed a strong absorption band at 280 that related to the π-π* and n-π* transition of the CDs (C = C and C = O groups) [60]. Then CDs were coated with different amounts of Au NPs. The UV–vis spectra of Au1-4-CDs were illustrated in Fig. 2b, which displayed an absorption band at 280 nm; moreover, another absorption band appeared in the visible region (550–565 nm). The intensity of the new absorption band significantly increased withincreasing the Au concentration (Fig. 2b inset). The appearance of these new bands confirmed the presence of Au NPs. Fig. 2c shows a photograph of the different Au1-4-CDs and the CDs solution, which showed variations of the solution color based on the Au concentration.

The emission spectrum of CDs was recorded under an excitation wavelength of 390 nm (Fig. 3 a). The fluorescence spectrum showed an emission band at 425 nm, which is in excellent agreement with the previously reported data [61,62]. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of CDs decorated with different Au NPs were recorded under the same conditions as shown in Fig. 3b, which showed decreased fluorescence intensity upon Au deposition onto the CDs. Moreover, a new emission band at 495 nm was observed, which confirmed the formation of Au NPs-CDs composite.

The capability of pure CDs to act as a DA sensor was investigated. Serial DA concentrations in PBS (pH 7) were prepared and mixed with the CDs solution for 1 min and then its fluorescence emission was recorded. Fig. 4 a shows the interaction of different DA concentrations within a range from 0.1 µM to 6 µM and their effect on the CDs' emission. The fluorescence spectra showed a decrease in the fluorescence intensity with increasing the DA concentration. The interaction between CDs and DA molecules could be due to physical interaction that results in quenching the CDs' luminescence. To study the effect of the DA concentration on the intensity of CDs' emission, the relationship between the DA concentration and ((F0/F) − 1) was represented in Fig. 4b, where F0 and F are the CDs' fluorescence intensity in the absence and existence of DA. Fig. 4b indicates the dependence of the CDs' fluorescence intensity on the DA concentration in the micromole scale. These results confirmed the capability of CDs to act as DA sensors but on a high concentration scale, which is higher than the normal DA levels in the human body. Therefore, a more sensitive platform is needed to achieve the biological DA level.

Fig. 5 a shows the emission spectra of DA after interacting with different Au1-4@CDs exited with a leaser of 240 nm. The results showed aset of emission bands that confirmed the interaction of DA with Au1-4@CDs. The intensity of the emission bands was significantly decreased with increasing the Au concentrationused for decorating CDs until Au3@CDs. Moreover, the use of Au4@CDs showed a slight reduction in the emission intensity compared to Au3@CDs. Thus, Au4@CDs were used for further measurements.

The efficiency of Au NPs-CDs for monitoring the DA levels was studied. Fig. 5b shows the effects of DA at the nanomole scale on the Au NPs-CDs' fluorescence intensity. The equivalent amounts of DA solution were mixed with Au NPs-CDs to prepare the desired DA concentrations. The solution was mixed for 1 min, then the emission spectrum was directly recorded. Fig. 5b shows the quenching of the Au NPs-CDs emission upon interaction with DA molecules. It is worth noting that the emission quenching of Au NPs-CDs was increased with increasing the DA concentration, which confirmed the dependence of the Au NPs-CDs' fluorescence intensity is dependent on the DA concentration and confirmed the interaction between Au NPs-CDs and DA. According to the previous studies [61,63,64], the binding between DA and Au NPs-CDs is based on the electrostatic and π-π attraction forces between the amino groups of the DA molecules and the Au NPs. The adsorbed DA molecules could be easily oxidized in the aqueous solution. DA into the oxidation electron acceptor o-quinone moieties. Excitation of Au NPs-CDs will cause electron transfer from Au NPs to the o-quinone moieties hence quenching the Au NPs-CDs' fluorescence intensity. Thus, the quenching of the Au NPs-CDs' fluorescence intensity is based on the DA concentration.

Fig. 5c shows the relationship between the DA concentration and the (F0/F − 1), where F0 and F are the Au NPs-CDs' fluorescence intensity in the absence and existence of DA. The relationship between DA concentration and the ((F0/F) − 1) showed a linear curve with R2 of 0.9947. The limit of detection (LOD) and quantification limit (LOQ) values of the developed Au NPs-CDs toward detection of DA were calculated to be 2.04 nM and 6.18 nM. The sensitivity of the proposed DA sensor was compared with the previously reported sensors either based on absorption or emission spectra (Table 1 ). The results indicated the high sensitivity of the proposed sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and reproducibility of Au NPs-CDs toward detection of DA
ce:section.ce:para: The overlap of the oxidation potential of DA with that of AA and UA interferences is the main challenge, facing the development of DA electrochemical sensors. Thus, an investigation of the efficacy of the proposed sensor for monitoring DA in the presence of these interferences, which are present in human fluids is necessary. Fig. 6 a shows the effects of 10 µM of AA, UA, glucose, and a mixture of these three interferences on the Au NPs-CDs' fluorescence. The results showed that these materials do not affect the Au NPs-CDs' fluorescence. Furthermore, the addition of 1 µM DA to the mixture of 10 µM glucose, AA, and UA cause a significant quenching of the Au NPs-CDs' fluorescence. The above results indicated the capability of the proposed sensors to detect DA in the existence of different interferences.

The reproducibility of the developed sensors was investigated as follows, three batches of the fabricated Au NPs-CDs were used for DA detection. Fig. 6b shows the fluorescence spectra of DA after interacting with three batches of the fabricated Au NPs-CDs. The results affirm that the three fabricated Au NPs-CDs batches show the same emission bands. The relative standard deviation (RSD) is found to be 2.7 %, which confirms the good reproducibility of the fabricated Au NPs-CDs.

The application of the proposed sensor for DA monitoring is essentially to study its efficiency. To study the Au NPs-CDs efficiency to sense DA in real samples, 5 concentrations of DA were prepared in human serum dissolved in PBS (pH 7). The DA-spiked human serum solutions were used and the Au NPs-CDs' fluorescence spectra were recorded at 1 min of treatment. Fig. 7 shows the Au NPs-CDs' fluorescence spectra after treatment with different DA concentrations in human serum samples. The recovery percentages were calculated and tabled in Table 2 . The results showed a good recovery, which indicated the high efficacy of the proposed sensor for monitoring DA in such a complicated matrix.

Here, we fabricated CDs based on the green method and followed by their modification with Au NPs. The fabricated Au NPs-CDs nanocomposite was utilized for detecting dopamine neurotransmitters as biomarkers for several nervous diseases based on quenching the fluorescence intensity. The Au NPs-CDs sensor showed the ability to detect DA within concentrations ranging from 20 nM to 200 nM with an LOD of 2.04 nM and LOQ of 6.18 nM. Moreover, the Au NPs-CDs sensor was successfully utilized for detecting DA in the presence of several interferences as well as in human serum samples. Thus, the developed sensor has the efficiency to sense DA in complex matrixes. The current proposed sensor showed many advantages including simple, easy-to-use, selective, and susceptible DA sensor. In addition, the developed Au NPs-CDs could open the door for a wide range of sensing applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the University of Jeddah, Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, under grant No. (UJ-23-DR-271). Therefore, the authors thank the University of Jeddah for its technical and financial support
